docs.content:
Domain: cheatSheets
Concept: Data Exploration with Pandas
Detail: Quick reference for pandas data exploration. Import: `import pandas as pd`. Load: `pd.read_csv('file.csv')`. View: `.head(n=5)`. Types: `.dtypes`. Counts: `.value_counts()`. Stats: `.describe()`. DataFrame is 2D table, Series is 1D column. Access single column: `df['col']` (Series), multiple: `df[['a','b']]` (DataFrame). Normalize counts: `.value_counts(normalize=True)`.
Context: Python | python, pandas, data-science, learning

Domain: cheatSheets
Concept: Python Methods & Lists
Detail: String methods: `.lower()` converts to lowercase. List methods: `.sort()` sorts ascending in-place, `.append(item)` adds to end. Parameter `reverse=True` for descending sort. Methods use parentheses (actions), attributes don't (stored info). List manipulation: `my_list.sort(reverse=True)`. String: `category.lower()`.
Context: Python | python, pandas, data-science, learning

Domain: cheatSheets
Concept: Standard Deviation
Detail: Measure of spread returning units to original scale via square root of variance. Calculate: `np.std(data)`. Manual: `variance ** 0.5`. Z-Score formula: `(x - μ) / σ` gives number of standard deviations from mean. Symbol: σ. Requires variance calculation as prerequisite. Related: Z-Score, outlier detection, normal distribution.
Context: DataScience | statistics, data-science, descriptive-statistics, learning

Domain: cheatSheets
Concept: Variance
Detail: Numerical measure of spread that squares differences from mean to ensure positive values. Calculate: `np.var(data)`. Formula: `Σ(X - μ)² / N`. Symbol: σ². Small variance = clustered data near mean. Manual: compute mean, square differences, average them. Sample variance uses N-1 denominator. Related: standard deviation, mean, normal distribution.
Context: DataScience | variance, statistics, data-science, descriptive-statistics, learning

metadata.source_file: quick:Deep Learning Fundamentals: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning using multi-layered neural networks. Key concepts: "Deep" = multiple layers learning progressively complex patterns. Mimics brain structure. Requires massive data + GPUs. Applications: language models (ChatGPT), self-driving cars, facial recognition. Trade-offs: accurate with large datasets, learns from raw data, but needs huge data, computationally expensive, black-box decisions. History: 1957 Perceptron (Rosenblatt), 2012 deep CNN won ImageNet by 10.8%. Neural networks teach computers pattern recognition. Core math: scalars, vectors, matrices, tensors. Forward propagation, loss functions (MSE, cross-entropy), backpropagation, gradient descent, learning rate.

# Deep Learning Fundamentals: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning using multi-layered neural networks. Key concepts: "Deep" = multiple layers learning progressively complex patterns. Mimics brain structure. Requires massive data + GPUs. Applications: language models (ChatGPT), self-driving cars, facial recognition. Trade-offs: accurate with large datasets, learns from raw data, but needs huge data, computationally expensive, black-box decisions. History: 1957 Perceptron (Rosenblatt), 2012 deep CNN won ImageNet by 10.8%. Neural networks teach computers pattern recognition. Core math: scalars, vectors, matrices, tensors. Forward propagation, loss functions (MSE, cross-entropy), backpropagation, gradient descent, learning rate.

Deep Learning Fundamentals: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning using multi-layered neural networks. Key concepts: "Deep" = multiple layers learning progressively complex patterns. Mimics brain structure. Requires massive data + GPUs. Applications: language models (ChatGPT), self-driving cars, facial recognition. Trade-offs: accurate with large datasets, learns from raw data, but needs huge data, computationally expensive, black-box decisions. History: 1957 Perceptron (Rosenblatt), 2012 deep CNN won ImageNet by 10.8%. Neural networks teach computers pattern recognition. Core math: scalars, vectors, matrices, tensors. Forward propagation, loss functions (MSE, cross-entropy), backpropagation, gradient descent, learning rate.

metadata.title: Neural Network Architecture: Three layer types — Input Layer (1, each node = one feature), Hidden Layers (0 to many, add complexity and learn patterns), Output Layer (1, final prediction). Components: Nodes/Neurons hold values, Weights are learning parameters connecting nodes (adjusted during training), Bias added to weighted sum. Forward Propagation: Input → [Weighted Sum → Activation] → Hidden → Output. At each layer: weighted_sum = (inputs · weights_transpose) + bias, then output = Activation(weighted_sum). Activation Functions: ReLU (hidden layers, most popular, introduces nonlinearity), Sigmoid (output, binary classification), Softmax (output, multi-class). Without activation functions, multiple layers = just linear regression.

docs.content:
# Neural Network Architecture: Three layer types — Input Layer (1, each node = one feature), Hidden Layers (0 to many, add complexity and learn patterns), Output Layer (1, final prediction). Components: Nodes/Neurons hold values, Weights are learning parameters connecting nodes (adjusted during training), Bias added to weighted sum. Forward Propagation: Input → [Weighted Sum → Activation] → Hidden → Output. At each layer: weighted_sum = (inputs · weights_transpose) + bias, then output = Activation(weighted_sum). Activation Functions: ReLU (hidden layers, most popular, introduces nonlinearity), Sigmoid (output, binary classification), Softmax (output, multi-class). Without activation functions, multiple layers = just linear regression.

Neural Network Architecture: Three layer types — Input Layer (1, each node = one feature), Hidden Layers (0 to many, add complexity and learn patterns), Output Layer (1, final prediction). Components: Nodes/Neurons hold values, Weights are learning parameters connecting nodes (adjusted during training), Bias added to weighted sum. Forward Propagation: Input → [Weighted Sum → Activation] → Hidden → Output. At each layer: weighted_sum = (inputs · weights_transpose) + bias, then output = Activation(weighted_sum). Activation Functions: ReLU (hidden layers, most popular, introduces nonlinearity), Sigmoid (output, binary classification), Softmax (output, multi-class). Without activation functions, multiple layers = just linear regression.

metadata.source_file: quick:Neural Network Training Process: 1) Forward Propagation → make prediction, 2) Calculate Loss → how wrong are we?, 3) Backpropagation → calculate gradients, 4) Gradient Descent → update weights, 5) Repeat. Loss Functions: MSE for regression (predicting numbers), Cross-Entropy for classification (predicting categories). Backpropagation: going backward through network to calculate how much each weight contributed to error. Gradient = rate of change of loss w.r.t. each weight. Gradient Descent formula: parameter_new = parameter_old + learning_rate × gradient(loss). Learning rate: too small = slow/stuck, too large = overshoots, just right = efficient. Variants: GD (all data, slow, smooth), SGD (1 random point, fast, noisy), Mini-batch (32-128 samples, balanced, most common), Adam (adaptive per-parameter, most popular).

metadata.source_file: quick:Machine Learning Taxonomy & Roadmap: Three branches — Supervised Learning (labeled data): Classical ML (Linear/Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, SVM) and Deep Learning (Dense Networks, RNNs/LSTM, CNNs, Transformers). Unsupervised Learning (no labels): Clustering (K-means, DBSCAN), Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE), Anomaly Detection. Reinforcement Learning (learn from environment): Value-based (DQN), Policy-based (PPO, A2C), applications in Game AI (RLGym), Robotics, Trading. Key concepts: epochs (one pass through dataset), iterations (one weight update), batch (subset per iteration), hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size). Classical ML good for structured/tabular data. Deep learning excels with large datasets, complex patterns, raw data (images, audio, text).

metadata.title: Linear Algebra for Machine Learning: Data structures — Scalar (0D, single number), Vector (1D, array), Matrix (2D, rows × columns), Tensor (3D+, multidimensional). Matrix dimensions always Rows × Columns. Operations: Addition (element-wise), Scalar Multiplication (multiply all by constant), Matrix Multiplication (inner dimensions must match: (A×B)·(B×C)=(A×C), row-column dot products), Transpose (flip rows↔columns, essential for neural network calculations, aligns dimensions for multiplication). Why it matters: forward propagation is matrix multiplication — weighted_sum = (inputs · weights_transpose) + bias. NumPy: np.dot(A,B) for multiplication, A.T for transpose, A.shape for dimensions.
